text: So we're going to talk about putting in a chest tube, tube thoracostomy.
And when you do a tube thoracostomy, first you need to find your landmarks.
With this patient, the landmarks are a little difficult to identify because the tissue isn't very compliant.
But you want to ideally put the tube thoracostomy in the fourth or fifth intercostal space.
And typically, that'll line up to the nipple.
if you can't really palpate and feel.
But if you can palpate and feel, it's always better to kind of figure out where the fourth space is.
Another anatomical landmark thing that you can use is the triangle of safety, which is defined by the pec major muscle, which would come like that.
And then the latissimus dorsi muscle, which would come like that.
And then the fourth or fifth inner space would be the inferior boundary of the triangle of safety.
Ideally, when you're doing this, it would be really helpful to put the patient's arm up to spread the rib spaces apart so you have more to work with and more space to get into the chest.
Two-thoracoscopy is an extremely painful procedure.
It's really important to give local anesthesia.
So once we've identified where we're going to make our incision, so let's say...
The fourth inner space is approximately here and we want to go in the mid axillary line which is going to be somewhere right here.
We want to give plenty of local anesthesia to reduce as much pain as we can.
So the way that I usually do it is I give a little skin wheel, you know, give a little skin wheel and then infiltrate deeper.
And when you're infiltrating deeper, this will also give you, you know, kind of all around where you're going to incise.
So it will give you an idea of where the rib is, which will help you when you make your incision.
We're going to make an incision.

And when you make the incision, it's really important that you make a wide enough incision when you start because you don't want to struggle with your incision and have to go back and make it wider after you've already made one because there's going to be bleeding and it's going to become more difficult.
So we'll make...
a vertical incision, mid-axillary line, fourth inner space, about three or four centimeters.
So now we've got our incision.
What I like to do is I like to cut all the way down until I can feel the rib, which is what I can feel here.
And then you can use your finger to get in and actually feel where the rib is.
This is a rib right here.
So we've cut down to the rib.
And once we've found our rib, we want to go above the rib because the neurovascular bundle in the ribs is in the inferior aspect of each rib.

So if we go through the neurovascular bundle, we have possible complications like forming a hematoma there or uncontrolled bleeding from the intercostal artery, which can be problematic, which is why we try to go above the rib.
Since we're on the left side, obviously you've got the heart and you want to protect against injuring that.

So a technique that you could use is to guard the tip of your clamp with your finger so that once you pierce through the intercostal muscle into the thoracic cavity, this doesn't freely enter all the way.
So now we've got our incision.
We've got our clamp guarded.
Feel the superior part of the rib and you kind of want to slide over the superior aspect of the rib with your finger guarding and then the motion is going to be like that.
You just want to pierce into the thoracic cavity over the rib the whole time.
So go in and then I can feel that I'm in the thoracic cavity.
Once you're in the thoracic cavity,
Put your fingers in the clamp, open it, and spread as wide as you can.
And once you're fully open and spread, pull out.
Okay, so now we've got our tract entering the thoracic cavity, and then you put your finger inside and sweep to see if you can feel the lung.
I can feel lung really well.
And at this point, when you're doing it in a live patient with a pneumothorax or a hemothorax, you would expect to get a large gush of blood or air or both, which would also help you
confirm that you're in the right place.
So with the chest tube we're going to pass this rounded end and to pass the chest tube, when you pass the chest tube in you want it to be positioned posteriorly and you want it to go cranially.
So what you can do is kind of grab the end of it with your clam

and you can use your finger to help you guide it and another important thing if you have another clamp is to clamp this end because if it's a large hemothorax and you pass this in blood is going to start pouring out of this end again we have our hole
Use your finger to guide you.
And it's important that you, like I said before, that you try to go posterior and cranially.
Once you're in the cavity and you're pointing down that way, come off your clamp and then pass your two.
And you need to make sure that all of your holes in the beginning, your sentinel holes, are all inside the thoracic cavity or else the chest tube won't function.
So to secure your tube, you can use a straight needle or a curved needle.
In a trauma situation, sometimes a straight needle is more useful if you don't have a needle holder.
Go ahead and get in.
Take your bites.
Probably better to do one at a time with this so that you don't
injure yourself.
Get your needle off to reduce chances of injuring yourself.
Your first stitch is going to be to close up the incision, the lower part of the incision that you made.
And I would throw three knots just to make sure that it's real secure.
And then once you put that first stitch in, you need to actually secure the tube.
So there are many ways to do it.
You can wrap it around multiple times.
And then tie it down.

There's also another technique where you kind of make a U, so you have your two strings here, make a U like this, pass this one through, that'll kind of cinch down on it, and you can do the same thing with the other side.
So that'll cinch it down, and then tie.
And you need to make sure that your knots are tight, or else you're not really securing anything.
And then we have a chest tube, and it's very secure.
